messages.content:
Where is a sample of deoxygenated blood likely found in the circulatory system of the human body? 

Group of answer choices

At the beginning of the systemic circuit

At the end of the systemic circuit

At the beginning of the pulmonary circuit

At the end of the pulmonary circuit

It is impossible to say with only this information.
At the beginning of the pulmonary circuit

What of the following valves prevents backflow of the blood into the left atrium when the left ventricle contracts?  

Group of answer choices

Mitral Valve

Aortic Valve

Tricuspid Valve

Pulmonary Valve
Mitral Valve

How does the body respond when you need more blood, e.g. during a period of intense exercise? 

Group of answer choices

Increase cardiac output

Body cannot respond to this situation

Body forced to stop and rest

Increase blood pressure
Increase cardiac output

What is normal ejection fraction? 

Group of answer choices

75%

100%

50%

25%
The normal ejection fraction is typically around 55-70%.

Increased sympathetic to the heart will likely result in 

Group of answer choices

decreased heart rate and increased cardiac output

increased heart rate and increased cardiac output

decreased heart rate and decreased cardiac output

increased heart rate and decreased cardiac output
Increased sympathetic stimulation to the heart will likely result in increased heart rate and increased cardiac output.

Which of the following molecules diffuse IN to most capillaries 

Group of answer choices

carbon dioxide and water

oxygen and nutrients

carbon dioxide and red blood cells

oxygen and water
Oxygen and nutrients typically diffuse into most capillaries.

Which of the following are not supplied by branches of the thoracic aorta?

Group of answer choices

diaphragm

oesophagus

lungs

kidneys
The lungs are not supplied by branches of the thoracic aorta.

A 55 year old man feels that he gets more breathless on climbing stairs.  He sees a cardiologist for advice. 

Which of the following investigation would a cardiologist do to evaluate the heart function.  

Group of answer choices

Arterial blood gas

Electrocardiogram

Transthoracic echo

Coronary angiogram
To evaluate the heart function, a cardiologist would likely perform a Transthoracic echo (echocardiogram).

A 60 year old man was found collapsed on the floor in a park.  First Aid responders on the scene found the man to have no pulse and started chest compressions immediately.  Why might the chest compressions help this man?

Chest compressions in this situation would help because they can generate artificial circulation and manually pump blood to vital organs, including the brain. This can temporarily restore oxygenation of tissues and buy time until further medical interventions can be provided.
Trace the flow of blood from the heart through the systemic circulation and back to the heart.  How is a uni-directional blood flow maintained in the systemic circulation?

The flow of blood from the heart through the systemic circulation and back to the heart can be traced as follows:

1. The blood is pumped out of the left ventricle of the heart into the aorta.
2. The aorta carries oxygenated blood to smaller arteries, arterioles, and eventually to capillaries throughout the body's organs and tissues.
3. At the capillaries, the exchange of oxygen, nutrients, and waste products occurs.
4. Deoxygenated blood returns from the capillaries into venules, which then merge into larger veins.
5. The veins carry deoxygenated blood back towards the heart.
6. The superior and inferior vena cava deliver the deoxygenated blood to the right atrium of the heart.
7. From the right atrium, blood flows into the right ventricle.
8. The right ventricle contracts and pumps the deoxygenated blood into the pulmonary trunk.
9. The pulmonary trunk splits into the left and right pulmonary arteries, which carry the blood to the lungs.
10. In the lungs, the blood undergoes oxygenation and gets rid of carbon dioxide.
11. Oxygenated blood is then returned to the heart through the pulmonary veins.
12. The pulmonary veins deliver the oxygenated blood to the left atrium of the heart.
13. From the left atrium, blood flows into the left ventricle.
14. Finally, the left ventricle contracts, pumping oxygenated blood back into the aorta, completing the cycle.

To maintain a unidirectional blood flow in the systemic circulation, the heart utilizes one-way valves in the blood vessels. These valves ensure that blood flows in the desired direction and prevents backward flow (regurgitation). The valves, such as the aortic valve and mitral valve, open to allow blood to pass through and then close to prevent backflow. This mechanism helps maintain the desired flow of blood throughout the systemic circulation.
